volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Hecuba (Section 1129 - Section 1281)

sections.label: Section 1129
sections.original: ἴσχʼ · ἐκβαλὼν δὲ καρδίας τὸ βάρβαρον
sections.translation: Hold ! banish that savage spirit from your heart

sections.label: Section 1130
sections.original: λέγʼ , ὡς ἀκούσας σοῦ τε τῆσδέ τʼ ἐν μέρει
sections.translation: and plead your cause , so that after hearing you and her in turn I may fairly decide what reason there is for your present sufferings .

sections.label: Section 1132
sections.original: λέγοιμʼ ἄν . ἦν τις Πριαμιδῶν νεώτατος ,
sections.translation: I will tell my tale . There was a son of Priam , Polydorus , the youngest , a child by Hecuba , whom his father Priam sent to me from Troy to bring up in my halls ,

sections.label: Section 1135
sections.original: ὕποπτος ὢν δὴ Τρωικῆς ἁλώσεως .
sections.translation: suspecting no doubt the fall of Troy . I killed him ; but hear my reason for killing him , how cleverly and wisely I had thought it out . My fear was that if that child were left to be your enemy , he would repeople Troy and settle it afresh ;

sections.label: Section 1140
sections.original: γνόντες δʼ Ἀχαιοὶ ζῶντα Πριαμιδῶν τινα
sections.translation: and the Achaeans , knowing that a son of Priam survived , might bring another expedition against the Phrygian land , and then harry and lay waste these plains of Thrace , for the neighbours of Troy to experience the very troubles we were lately suffering , O king .

sections.label: Section 1145
sections.original: Ἑκάβη δὲ παιδὸς γνοῦσα θανάσιμον μόρον
sections.translation: Now Hecuba , having discovered the death of her son , brought me here on the following pretext , saying she would tell me of hidden treasure stored up in Ilium by the race of Priam ; and she led me apart with my children into the tent , that no other might hear her news .

sections.label: Section 1150
sections.original: ἵζω δὲ κλίνης ἐν μέσῳ κάμψας γόνυ ·
sections.translation: So I sat down on a couch in their midst to rest ; for there were many of the Trojan maidens seated there , some on my right hand , some on my left , as if beside a friend ; and they were praising the weaving of our Edonian handiwork , looking at this robe as they held it up to the light ;

sections.label: Section 1155
sections.original: ἄλλαι δὲ κάμακα Θρῃκίαν θεώμεναι
sections.translation: while others examined my Thracian spear and so stripped me of two-fold protection . And those that were young mothers were dandling my children in their arms , with loud admiration , as they passed them on from hand to hand to remove them far from their father ;

sections.label: Section 1160
sections.original: κᾆτʼ ἐκ γαληνῶν — πῶς δοκεῖς ; — προσφθεγμάτων
sections.translation: and then after their smooth speeches — would you believe it ?— in an instant snatching daggers from somewhere in their dress they stab my children ; while others , like foes , seized me hand and foot ;

sections.label: Section 1165
sections.original: εἰ μὲν πρόσωπον ἐξανισταίην ἐμόν ,
sections.translation: and if I tried to raise my head , anxious to help my children , they would clutch me by the hair ; while if I stirred my hands , I could do nothing , poor wretch ! for the numbers of the women . At last they did a fearful deed , worse than what had gone before ; for they took their brooches

sections.label: Section 1170
sections.original: πόρπας λαβοῦσαι , τὰς ταλαιπώρους κόρας
sections.translation: and stabbed the hapless pupils of my eyes , making them gush with blood , and then fled through the chambers ; up I sprang like a wild beast in pursuit of the shameless murderesses , searching along each wall with hunter ’ s care ,

sections.label: Section 1175
sections.original: βάλλων ἀράσσων . τοιάδε σπεύδων χάριν
sections.translation: dealing buffets , spreading ruin . This then is what I have suffered because of my zeal for you , Agamemnon , for slaying an enemy of yours . But to spare you a lengthy speech , if any of the men of former times have spoken ill of women , if any does so now , or shall do so hereafter ,

sections.label: Section 1180
sections.original: ἅπαντα ταῦτα συντεμὼν ἐγὼ φράσω ·
sections.translation: I will say all this in one short sentence ; for neither land or sea produces such a race , as whoever has had to do with them knows .

sections.label: Section 1183
sections.original: μηδὲν θρασύνου μηδὲ τοῖς σαυτοῦ κακοῖς
sections.translation: Curb your bold tongue , and do not , because of your own woes , thus embrace the whole race of women in one reproach .

sections.label: Section 1185
sections.original: πολλαὶ γὰρ ἡμῶν , αἳ μέν εἰσʼ ἐπίφθονοι ,
sections.translation: For though some of us , and those a numerous class , deserve to be disliked , there are others among us who rank naturally among the good .

sections.label: Section 1187
sections.original: Ἀγάμεμνον , ἀνθρώποισιν οὐκ ἐχρῆν ποτε
sections.translation: Never ought words to have outweighed deeds in this world , Agamemnon . No ! if a man ’ s deeds were good , so should his words have been ;

sections.label: Section 1190
sections.original: εἴτʼ αὖ πονηρά , τοὺς λόγους εἶναι σαθρούς ,
sections.translation: if , on the other hand , evil , his words should have been unsound , instead of its being possible at times to speak injustice well . There are , it is true , clever persons , who have made a science of this , but their cleverness cannot last for ever ; a miserable end awaits them ; no one ever yet escaped .

sections.label: Section 1195
sections.original: καί μοι τὸ μὲν σὸν ὧδε φροιμίοις ἔχει ·
sections.translation: This part of my prelude belongs to you . Now will I turn to this fellow , and will give you your answer , you who say it was to save Achaea double toil and for Agamemnon ’ s sake that you killed my son . No , villain , in the first place

sections.label: Section 1200
sections.original: τὸ βάρβαρον γένοιτʼ ἂν Ἕλλησιν γένος
sections.translation: the barbarian race would never be friends with Hellas , nor could it be . Again , what interest did you have to further by your zeal ? was it to form some marriage , or on the score of kinship , or what reason ? or was it likely that they would sail here again and destroy

sections.label: Section 1205
sections.original: πλεύσαντες αὖθις ; τίνα δοκεῖς πείσειν τάδε ;
sections.translation: your country ’ s crops ? Whom do you expect to persuade into believing that ? If you would only speak the truth , it was the gold that slew my son , and your greedy spirit . Now tell me this : why , when Troy was victorious , when her ramparts still stood round her ,

sections.label: Section 1210
sections.original: ἔζη τε Πρίαμος Ἕκτορός τʼ ἤνθει δόρυ ,
sections.translation: when Priam was alive , and Hector ’ s warring prospered , why did you not then , if you were really minded to do Agamemnon a service , slay the child , for you had him in your palace beneath your care , or bring him with you alive to the Argives ? But when the enemy took us from the light

sections.label: Section 1215
sections.original: καπνῷ δʼ ἐσήμηνʼ ἄστυ — πολεμίων ὕπο ,
sections.translation: and our city made a signal by its smoke , you murdered the guest who had come to your hearth .

sections.label: Section 1217
sections.original: πρὸς τοῖσδε νῦν ἄκουσον , ὡς φανῇς κακός .
sections.translation: Furthermore , to prove your villainy , hear this ; if you were really a friend to those Achaeans , you should have brought the gold , which you say you are keeping not for yourself but for this man ,

sections.label: Section 1220
sections.original: δοῦναι φέροντα πενομένοις τε καὶ χρόνον
sections.translation: and given it to them , for they were in need and had endured a long exile from their native land . But not even now can you bring yourself to part with it , but persist in keeping it in your palace . Again , had you kept my son safe and sound , as your duty was ,

sections.label: Section 1225
sections.original: σώσας τε τὸν ἐμόν , εἶχες ἂν καλὸν κλέος ·
sections.translation: a fair renown would have been your reward , for it is in trouble ’ s hour that the good most clearly show their friendship ; though prosperity by itself in every case finds friends . If you were in need of money and he were prosperous , that son of mine would have been as a mighty treasure for you to draw upon ;

sections.label: Section 1230
sections.original: νῦν δʼ οὔτʼ ἐκεῖνον ἄνδρʼ ἔχεις σαυτῷ φίλον ,
sections.translation: but now you have him no longer to be your friend , and the benefit of the gold is gone from you , your children too , and you yourself are in this sorry plight . I say to you , Agamemnon , if you help this man , you will show your worthlessness ; for you will be serving a guest-friend neither pious nor to be trusted where he should be ,

sections.label: Section 1235
sections.original: οὐχ ὅσιον , οὐ δίκαιον εὖ δράσεις ξένον ·
sections.translation: not devout , not just ; while I shall say you delight in evil-doers , being such a one yourself ; but I do not abuse my masters .

sections.label: Section 1238
sections.original: φεῦ φεῦ · βροτοῖσιν ὡς τὰ χρηστὰ πράγματα
sections.translation: Ah ! how a good cause always affords men an opening for a good speech .

sections.label: Section 1240
sections.original: ἀχθεινὰ μέν μοι τἀλλότρια κρίνειν κακά ,
sections.translation: To be judge in a stranger ’ s troubles goes much against my grain , but still I must ; yes , for to take this matter in hand and then put it from me is a shameful course . My opinion , that you may know it , is that it was not for the sake of the Achaeans or me that you killed your guest ,

sections.label: Section 1245
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ὡς ἔχῃς τὸν χρυσὸν ἐν δόμοισι σοῖς .
sections.translation: but to keep that gold in your own house . In your trouble you make a case in your own interests . Perhaps among you it is a light thing to murder guests , but with us in Hellas it is a disgrace . How can I escape reproach if I judge you not guilty ?

sections.label: Section 1250
sections.original: οὐκ ἂν δυναίμην . ἀλλʼ ἐπεὶ τὰ μὴ καλὰ
sections.translation: I could not . No , since you endured your horrid crime , endure as well its painful consequence .

sections.label: Section 1252
sections.original: οἴμοι , γυναικός , ὡς ἔοιχʼ , ἡσσώμενος
sections.translation: Woe is me ! worsted by a woman and a slave , I am , it seems , to suffer by unworthy hands .

sections.label: Section 1254
sections.original: οὔκουν δικαίως , εἴπερ εἰργάσω κακά ;
sections.translation: Is it not just for your atrocious crime ?

sections.label: Section 1255
sections.original: οἴμοι τέκνων τῶνδʼ ὀμμάτων τʼ ἐμῶν , τάλας .
sections.translation: Ah , my children ! ah , my blinded eyes ! woe is me !

sections.label: Section 1256
sections.original: ἀλγεῖς · τί δʼ ; ἦ ʼμὲ παιδὸς οὐκ ἀλγεῖν δοκεῖς ;
sections.translation: You grieve ; but what of me ? Do you think I do not grieve for my son ?

sections.label: Section 1257
sections.original: χαίρεις ὑβρίζουσʼ εἰς ἔμʼ , ὦ πανοῦργε σύ ;
sections.translation: You wicked wretch ! Is your delight in mocking me ?

sections.label: Section 1258
sections.original: οὐ γάρ με χαίρειν χρή σε τιμωρουμένην ;
sections.translation: Yes , for I am avenged on you ; have I not cause for delight ?

sections.label: Section 1259
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὐ τάχʼ , ἡνίκʼ ἄν σε ποντία νοτὶς —
sections.translation: It will soon cease , when ocean ’ s flood —

sections.label: Section 1260
sections.original: μῶν ναυστολήσῃ γῆς ὅρους Ἑλληνίδος ;
sections.translation: Shall convey me to the shores of Hellas ?

sections.label: Section 1261
sections.original: κρύψῃ μὲν οὖν πεσοῦσαν ἐκ καρχησίων .
sections.translation: No , but will close over you when you fall from the masthead .

sections.label: Section 1262
sections.original: πρὸς τοῦ βιαίων τυγχάνουσαν ἁλμάτων ;
sections.translation: Who will force me to take the leap ?

sections.label: Section 1263
sections.original: αὐτὴ πρὸς ἱστὸν ναὸς ἀμβήσῃ ποδί .
sections.translation: Of your own accord you will climb the ship ’ s mast .

sections.label: Section 1264
sections.original: ὑποπτέροις νώτοισιν ἢ ποίῳ τρόπῳ ;
sections.translation: With wings upon my back , or by what means ?

sections.label: Section 1265
sections.original: κύων γενήσῃ πύρσʼ ἔχουσα δέργματα .
sections.translation: You will become a dog with bloodshot gaze .

sections.label: Section 1266
sections.original: πῶς δʼ οἶσθα μορφῆς τῆς ἐμῆς μετάστασιν ;
sections.translation: How did you know of my transformation ?

sections.label: Section 1267
sections.original: ὁ Θρῃξὶ μάντις εἶπε Διόνυσος τάδε .
sections.translation: Dionysus , our Thracian prophet , told me so .

sections.label: Section 1268
sections.original: σοὶ δʼ οὐκ ἔχρησεν οὐδὲν ὧν ἔχεις κακῶν ;
sections.translation: And did he prophesy to you nothing of your present trouble ?

sections.label: Section 1269
sections.original: οὐ γάρ ποτʼ ἂν σύ μʼ εἷλες ὧδε σὺν δόλῳ .
sections.translation: No , for you would never have caught me thus by guile .

sections.label: Section 1270
sections.original: θανοῦσα δʼ ἢ ζῶσʼ ἐνθάδʼ ἐκπλήσω βίον ;
sections.translation: Dead or alive shall I complete my life here ?

sections.label: Section 1271
sections.original: θανοῦσα · τύμβῳ δʼ ὄνομα σῷ κεκλήσεται —
sections.translation: Dead ; and to your tomb shall be given a name —;

sections.label: Section 1272
sections.original: μορφῆς ἐπῳδόν , ἢ τί , τῆς ἐμῆς ἐρεῖς ;
sections.translation: Recalling my form , or what will you tell me ?

sections.label: Section 1273
sections.original: κυνὸς ταλαίνης σῆμα , ναυτίλοις τέκμαρ .
sections.translation: The hapless hound ’ s grave , a mark for mariners .

sections.label: Section 1274
sections.original: οὐδὲν μέλει μοι σοῦ γέ μοι δόντος δίκην .
sections.translation: It is nothing to me , now that you have paid me the penalty .

sections.label: Section 1275
sections.original: καὶ σήν γʼ ἀνάγκη παῖδα Κασάνδραν θανεῖν .
sections.translation: Further , your daughter Cassandra must die .

sections.label: Section 1276
sections.original: ἀπέπτυσʼ · αὐτῷ ταῦτα σοὶ δίδωμʼ ἔχειν .
sections.translation: I scorn the prophecy ! I give it to you to keep for yourself .

sections.label: Section 1277
sections.original: κτενεῖ νιν ἡ τοῦδʼ ἄλοχος , οἰκουρὸς πικρά .
sections.translation: The wife of Agamemnon , grim keeper of his palace , shall slay her .

sections.label: Section 1278
sections.original: μήπω μανείη Τυνδαρὶς τοσόνδε παῖς .
sections.translation: Never may the daughter of Tyndareus do such a frantic deed !

sections.label: Section 1279
sections.original: καὐτόν γε τοῦτον , πέλεκυν ἐξάρασʼ ἄνω .
sections.translation: And she shall slay this man as well , lifting high the axe .

sections.label: Section 1280
sections.original: οὗτος σύ , μαίνῃ καὶ κακῶν ἐρᾷς τυχεῖν ;
sections.translation: You creature , are you mad ? are you so eager to find sorrow ?

sections.label: Section 1281
sections.original: κτεῖνʼ , ὡς ἐν Ἄργει φόνια λουτρά σʼ ἀμμένει .
sections.translation: Kill me , for in Argos there awaits you a murderous bath .
